Thought: Checkpoint 1 conclusions: I have identified ColumnTransformer._hstack, _add_prefix_for_feature_names_out, _iter, and related logic. The bug arises because when a transformer has empty column selection, it might be omitted from self._iter(fitted=True) when replace_strings=True, causing mismatch between Xs and names_out.

I'll finish Checkpoint 1 by stating the bug and primary method responsible.
Now I'll write the Checkpoint 1 findings.
Checkpoint 1: Identify the bug

Reasoning before tool call:
- I need to extract the relevant entities (class/method names and locations) mentioned in the report so I can find them in the codebase and inspect the implementation.

Actions & results:
- I ran classify_report on the bug report. It identified ColumnTransformer, Pipeline, _column_transformer.py, methods: _iter, _add_prefix_for_feature_names_out, _hstack, fit_transform, fit, transform, and the stack trace lines referencing sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py.

Next I searched the codebase for ColumnTransformer and opened its source file to inspect relevant methods and context:
- I opened sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py and read it fully (lines 1–1000; file length 1131). I also inspected sklearn/utils/metaestimators._BaseComposition and sklearn/utils/_set_output.py because set_output(transform="pandas") is involved.

What I inspected in the code:
- ColumnTransformer class and its methods: __init__, _iter, _get_feature_name_out_for_transformer, get_feature_names_out, _add_prefix_for_feature_names_out, _fit_transform, fit_transform, transform, _hstack, _update_fitted_transformers, _record_output_indices, _validate_output, plus helper functions _is_empty_column_selection and _check_X.
- _BaseComposition (parent of ColumnTransformer) to understand inherited behavior.
- _set_output mixin and how methods are wrapped when set_output(transform="pandas") is used.

Findings (clear statement of the bug and primary responsible method/class):
- Symptom: When ColumnTransformer is used inside a Pipeline whose top-level .set_output(transform="pandas") is active, calling fit(X, y) fails with a pandas ValueError: Length mismatch: Expected axis has 3 elements, new values have 0 elements.
- Cause observed from code & stack trace: In ColumnTransformer._hstack, when the output configuration requests pandas DataFrame (config["dense"] == "pandas"), it concatenates the individual transformer outputs Xs (which are pandas objects) via pd.concat(Xs, axis=1) and then, if verbose_feature_names_out is True, builds transformer_names via self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) and feature_names_outs = [X.columns for X in Xs] and then computes names_out = self._add_prefix_for_feature_names_out(list(zip(transformer_names, feature_names_outs))). Finally it sets output.columns = names_out. The bug occurs because transformer_names (built from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)) can omit transformers whose column selection was empty and therefore were never fitted; whereas Xs and feature_names_outs are built from result of _fit_transform and include all Xs that correspond to the fitted transformers. This mismatch causes names_out length to not match the concatenated DataFrame columns length, leading pandas to raise a length mismatch error.
- Primary class/method responsible: sklearn.compose._column_transformer.ColumnTransformer, specifically its _hstack method (and indirectly _iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) and _update_fitted_transformers/_fit_transform) that produce inconsistent lists between Xs and transformer names used for building names_out. The helper _add_prefix_for_feature_names_out is being fed an inconsistent zipped list because the lists zipped don't align (some transformers omitted in one list).

Summary:
- The ColumnTransformer._hstack logic assumes that the sequence of transformers retrieved from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) corresponds one-to-one with the sequence of Xs passed in. But when a transformer has an empty column selection and thus is not fitted/omitted from fitted transformers, one of those lists may be shorter/longer than the other, causing a mismatch and the pandas ValueError when set_output("pandas") is active.

Checkpoint 4 — Propose a fix, then challenge it

Root cause summary (from your earlier analysis and confirmed by code review):
- ColumnTransformer._hstack builds names_out for pandas DataFrame outputs by zipping transformer_names and feature_names_outs (the latter collected from Xs). transformer_names is constructed with self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) while feature_names_outs is built from Xs (the actual outputs of transformers). In some code paths, especially after set_output operations at meta-estimator level, these two lists can become inconsistent in length/order (e.g., when a transformer had an empty column spec or when passthrough replacements changed), causing names_out length mismatches with the DataFrame columns and raising a pandas ValueError (Length mismatch).
- The central mismatch arises because self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) and the list of actual outputs Xs are not guaranteed to correspond 1:1 in the presence of empty column selections and the handling of 'drop'/'passthrough' in different places.

Proposed fix (high-level):
- Make the logic that builds column names in ColumnTransformer._hstack (and the related methods _validate_output and _record_output_indices which also assume a particular ordering) derive the transformer names in the same way the Xs were produced — i.e., use the fitted transformers information stored in self.transformers_ to obtain the sequence of transformer names that correspond to the produced Xs (filtering out 'drop' and empty selections). This ensures a one-to-one alignment between Xs and transformer names used to prefix feature names.
- Concretely, modify three methods in sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py:
  1. _validate_output: instead of creating names via self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) use the same non-empty-filtered order derived from self.transformers_.
  2. _record_output_indices: same change — iterate the non-dropped, non-empty entries of self.transformers_ (in the order in self.transformers_) to align with Xs.
  3. _hstack: when building transformer_names use the non-empty entries of self.transformers_ rather than self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True). Keep the existing fallback (use self._iter(...)) if self.transformers_ is not set for some reason to be defensive.
- This preserves existing behavior for many cases but avoids mismatch when self._iter/fitted=True/replace_strings=True ordering differs from Xs.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"

- The original code assumed that the sequence returned by self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) is always in exact 1:1 correspondence (in ordering and count) with the list of outputs Xs produced by _fit_transform / _transform. My fix changes that assumption: it assumes instead that self.transformers_ (the fitted transformers list) encodes the ordering of configured transformers and that filtering it for non-dropped and non-empty selections gives the correct sequence corresponding to Xs; _hstack and the other methods should use that to align names and outputs.

  - Use of self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) was a hardcoded path to build transformer names; replacing it removes that specific hardcoded assumption in favor of reading self.transformers_.

For component classes:
- FunctionTransformer: "one-to-one" sentinel is used as a named behavior for feature names — my fix does not change that.
- _set_output: the wrapper relies on estimator.get_feature_names_out for column names if columns is callable — my fix is careful to keep compatibility with get_feature_names_out by using it as a fallback.

For each hardcoded value:
- The "__" separator remains valid and unaffected by the change.
- _is_empty_column_selection: still appropriate — if the assumption that empty selections are encoded as empty lists or all-False boolean arrays changes, this check would need to be updated (but that is unrelated to this fix).
- Any integer offsets previously computed (e.g., output_indices_ slicing) are computed from Xs shapes and will not be impacted because _record_output_indices will now iterate transformer entries filtered from transformers_. The counts (n_columns) are taken from Xs, so any hardcoded numeric constants are not impacted.

Conclusion for 4b: The main hardcoded assumption I change is the use of self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) as the definitive source for transformer ordering corresponding to Xs. No numeric constant needs to become dynamic. The only additional dependency is on self.transformers_ existing and being the authoritative ordering; code is already created to populate transformers_ in _update_fitted_transformers during fit, so this is safe.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"

- We must check all complementary operations/methods in the parent class and elsewhere that might be affected by the change. The parent class _BaseComposition defines only parameter management methods (we re-read the file); it does not define transform logic that would be affected.

- ColumnTransformer inherits or defines methods that complement the change. I must re-read each relevant parent method and trace concrete variable values to ensure correctness.

Which parent's methods perform complementary operations?
- _BaseComposition doesn't define transform/get_feature_names_out, so no complementary method in parent to change.

But the protocol requires: For every inherited method that performs a complementary operation to what the fix changes, re-read the parent's full implementation and do a trace. The parent's relevant methods are just parameter helpers — no complementary transform logic. So nothing in parent needs overriding beyond what is already in ColumnTransformer.

However, the "reverse operation" in this context typically means: if we change how ColumnTransformer sets DataFrame columns (i.e., the naming during transform or fit_transform), do we need to change get_feature_names_out or any code that relies on the previous naming logic? get_feature_names_out itself relies on _iter(fitted=True) and _get_feature_name_out_for_transformer. My fix does not change get_feature_names_out; it only changes how _hstack creates names when getting DataFrame output. That could create slight differences in name selection in odd cases where a transformer produces a DataFrame but does not implement get_feature_names_out — _hstack's previous implementation used X.columns directly for each X and used self._iter(...) to obtain transformer names; my change uses self.transformers_ filtered to non-empty selections to get transformer_names. This keeps the same pattern of prefixing each column name with the correct transformer name.

Now per protocol: re-read any inherited methods that perform complementary operations. The key complementary methods are:

- get_feature_names_out (ColumnTransformer defines this). I must re-read its implementation (I already did) and trace through a scenario to ensure symmetry.

I will trace a concrete example:

Scenario (concrete substitution):

- Suppose ColumnTransformer with transformers = [("t1", Est1, [0]), ("t2", Est2, []), ("t3", Est3, [2,3])] and remainder='drop'.

  - Finally _hstack with config pandas will compute transformer_names via self.transformers_ filtered to non-empty => ["t1","t3"]. feature_names_outs = [X_t1.columns, X_t3.columns], lengths match. _add_prefix_for_feature_names_out will prefix each column by "t1__" for X_t1.columns and "t3__" for X_t3.columns. - For transform() (after fit):

  - Our new _hstack code uses self.transformers_ filtered to non-empty to obtain transformer names; this will match Xs ordering because during fit we stored the ordering of transformers_ consistent with how _fit_transform produced outputs. So transform case is handled.

Therefore the complementary operation (get_feature_names_out) remains consistent: get_feature_names_out uses self._iter(fitted=True) and _get_feature_name_out_for_transformer to produce feature names. But our _hstack uses X.columns when DataFrames are produced and derives transformer names from self.transformers_. Both paths produce the same prefixed names in typical cases. The only divergence is if an estimator implements get_feature_names_out in a way that diverges from X.columns for some transformer that returns a DataFrame; but that is expected: get_feature_names_out is the canonical API and may differ. We keep fallback logic: when transformer names count mismatch, we attempt to use get_feature_names_out() to set columns consistently.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"

I must go through each ColumnTransformer method and confirm it works with the change:

- __init__, _transformers property and setter, get_params, set_params — unaffected.

- set_output: unchanged. It calls super().set_output and then _safe_set_output on transformers. Our change does not alter set_output semantics.

- _iter: unchanged. But note: previously _hstack used self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True), now we avoid that in favor of filtering self.transformers_. _iter remains used elsewhere (e.g., in get_feature_names_out) and remains correct.

- _validate_transformers: unchanged.

- _validate_column_callables, _validate_remainder: unchanged.

- named_transformers_ property: unchanged.

- _get_feature_name_out_for_transformer and get_feature_names_out: unchanged. We must verify that get_feature_names_out still returns the same prefixed names as _hstack will set DataFrame columns to. Mostly yes; get_feature_names_out consults the transformer.get_feature_names_out when available and uses its output. Our _hstack constructs names from X.columns (the actual DataFrame columns). If a transformer returned a DataFrame but did not supply get_feature_names_out, ColumnTransformer._hstack will build names from X.columns and prefix with transformer name — which is what tests expect (see test_transformers_with_pandas_out_but_not_feature_names_out). So existing methods continue to work.

- _add_prefix_for_feature_names_out: unchanged.

- _update_fitted_transformers: unchanged; it sets self.transformers_ and _name_to_fitted_passthrough mapping.

- _validate_output: THIS IS CHANGED: we swapped the source of "names" to come from transformers_. The method originally iterated self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) for names; that could skip some transformers, leading to mismatch when zipping with result. The new approach will align correctly. It still validates 2D outputs and will raise if a transformer returns non-2D output. This still works — plus it's now robustly aligned.

- _record_output_indices: CHANGED similarly — now builds indices from transformers_. It retains existing behavior of setting empty slices for names not present. Works as intended.

- _log_message, _fit_transform, fit, fit_transform, transform: unchanged logic; they still call _hstack at the end; _hstack now uses self.transformers_ list, so outputs align.

- _hstack: CHANGED. Behavior:
  - Sparse output path unchanged.
  - Dense path: if config["dense"] == "pandas" and Xs are DataFrames, previously code used self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) to build transformer_names; now it uses self.transformers_. Behavior is the same in regular scenarios but robust in edge cases. There is a fallback in case of mismatch: try get_feature_names_out() and if that fails, leave column names alone. This prevents raising a pandas length mismatch error.

Therefore every existing method continues to work, with improved robustness. The only behavior change is a more robust method of constructing transformer names for DataFrame output; in extremely unusual cases (e.g., self.transformers_ missing, or mismatch), we fall back to existing get_feature_names_out path or no renaming.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"

- I do not think a new public method is needed. But we are changing how three internal methods derive transformer names. We must check whether any parent methods that rely on _iter(fitted=True) remain inconsistent. We examined get_feature_names_out — it uses _iter(fitted=True) and _get_feature_name_out_for_transformer and will continue to work. However, to ensure full symmetry, we considered whether get_feature_names_out should be changed to use self.transformers_ as well. But get_feature_names_out's design intentionally calls each transformer's get_feature_names_out, which may be necessary because some transformer may produce fewer/more feature names than columns on the DataFrame (they should normally match, or the transformer's get_feature_names_out should be authoritative). Changing get_feature_names_out to use self.transformers_ in a different way risks affecting tests that check get_feature_names_out behavior — so we should not change it.

Thus I conclude: no new method required. We only need to modify the three existing methods as described.

Edge cases to verify (and included in fixes):
- If self.transformers_ is not present (rarely, e.g., transform called on unfitted ColumnTransformer), we fall back to the old self._iter(...) approach to avoid raising new errors.
- If the length mismatch persists (some unexpected inconsistency), the fallback uses get_feature_names_out if available to compute names; if that fails, we simply return the concatenated DataFrame without renaming rather than letting pandas raise a "Length mismatch" ValueError. This avoids breaking user code and avoids raising a confusing pandas error; it is conservative.

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (complete analysis and patch plan)

Root cause (concise):
- ColumnTransformer._hstack builds DataFrame column names by pairing transformer names from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) with the column lists of the actual transformer outputs Xs. In some situations (e.g., empty column selections, passthrough replacements, set_output wrappers), these two sources can be inconsistent in length or ordering, leading to a mismatch between constructed names and DataFrame columns and causing pandas to raise a "Length mismatch" error. The code used two different ways to determine the sequence of transformers producing Xs; they were not guaranteed to be aligned in edge cases.

All locations that need to change (not just the primary bug):
1) sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py
   - _validate_output (lines ~609–623 in the file read)
     - Current behavior: builds 'names' using self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) and zips with result to validate the outputs.
     - Problem: names could be shorter/longer than result when self._iter and Xs are not aligned.
     - Fix: derive names from self.transformers_ filtered to exclude 'drop' and empty selections (use _is_empty_column_selection) so it aligns with Xs; fallback to self._iter(...) if transformers_ is not present.

   - _record_output_indices (lines ~624–645)
     - Current behavior: iterate enumerate(self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)) and use Xs[transformer_idx] to compute output indices. If self._iter yields a different sequence than Xs, index error or inconsistent mapping occurs.
     - Fix: iterate filtered self.transformers_ non-empty list as the authoritative sequence aligned to Xs; compute slices from Xs by index accordingly; keep existing logic to set empty slices for names not present; fallback to self._iter if transformers_ missing.

   - _hstack (lines ~824–876; specifically the DataFrame/pandas output branch)
     - Current behavior: when returning pandas DataFrame, if verbose_feature_names_out True it computes:
         transformer_names = [t[0] for t in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)]
         feature_names_outs = [X.columns for X in Xs]
         names_out = _add_prefix_for_feature_names_out(list(zip(transformer_names, feature_names_outs)))
         output.columns = names_out
       Then returns.
     - Problem: transformer_names and feature_names_outs may be mismatched in count/order in edge cases -> pandas ValueError.
     - Fix: derive transformer_names from self.transformers_ filtered to non-dropped, non-empty selections to align with Xs. If lengths match, proceed. If they don't, fallback to using get_feature_names_out() to compute names_out (which will raise if not possible), or else return output unchanged to avoid pandas length mismatch.
     - This requires also importing or referencing _is_empty_column_selection (already in same module).
     - This prevents the pandas "Length mismatch" error and produces correct column names in normal and edge cases.

Why these locations:
- _validate_output and _record_output_indices both assume the same alignment between Xs and self._iter(...). They must be updated to use the same alignment method as _hstack to prevent subtle inconsistencies and to ensure consistent output_indices_ mapping. Otherwise, we would still have mismatches between indices and DataFrame columns.

Any hardcoded values that need to become dynamic:
- No numeric hardcoded constants (counts, offsets) need to become dynamic because we already compute counts from Xs shapes. The only implicit assumption changed is which sequence to trust for transformer ordering; this is not a numeric constant, it's a source-of-truth change.

Any new methods or overrides required:
- No new public methods required. We need to modify the three methods above to make the logic consistent and robust.
- No override of parent methods required.
- No additional test-only helper methods required.

Behavioral and test consequences:
- Existing tests that rely on ColumnTransformer output column naming should observe same behavior in typical workflows.
- Edge-case tests (like ColumnTransformer in Pipeline with set_output("pandas") and transformers with empty selection) will no longer raise pandas "Length mismatch".
- Tests that examine get_feature_names_out should remain unaffected because get_feature_names_out is unchanged. Our _hstack still attempts to honor get_feature_names_out as a fallback.

Concrete patch summary (code-level changes):

In sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py:

- Replace in _validate_output:

    names = [
        name for name, _, _, _ in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)
    ]

  With robust derivation:

            name
            for name, trans, columns in self.transformers_
            if not (trans == "drop" or _is_empty_column_selection(columns))
        ]
    except AttributeError:

- Replace in _record_output_indices:

        self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)
    ):
        n_columns = Xs[transformer_idx].shape[1]
        self.output_indices_[name] = slice(idx, idx + n_columns)

  With:

        transformer_entries_non_empty = [
            (name, trans, columns)
            for name, trans, columns in self.transformers_
            if not (trans == "drop" or _is_empty_column_selection(columns))
        ]
    except AttributeError:
        transformer_entries_non_empty = [
            (name, trans, columns) for name, trans, columns, _ in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)
        ]

    for transformer_idx, (name, _, _) in enumerate(transformer_entries_non_empty):

- Replace in _hstack (pandas branch) the transformer_names derivation:

        t[0] for t in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)
    ]

===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug summary: ColumnTransformer produces a pandas DataFrame then sets its columns using a list of transformer names computed from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) while reading actual column lists from the transformer outputs Xs; those two lists can be misaligned (different length/order) when a transformer selection is empty or passthrough/replacement logic differs, causing pandas ValueError "Length mismatch" (sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py).
- Wrong assumption: the code assumes self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) always yields a 1:1 ordered sequence that matches the list of produced outputs Xs. That assumption is false in edge cases (empty selections, passthrough replacement, set_output wrapping).
- Root cause location: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py — primary symptom in ColumnTransformer._hstack (pandas branch); related incorrect assumptions also appear in ColumnTransformer._validate_output and ColumnTransformer._record_output_indices.
- Proposed fix strategy: derive the transformer sequence authoritative for produced Xs from self.transformers_ (the fitted transformers list) filtered to exclude 'drop' and empty selections (use _is_empty_column_selection). Use that sequence to align names with Xs in _hstack, and apply the same alignment in _validate_output and _record_output_indices. Provide defensive fallbacks: if lengths still mismatch, try get_feature_names_out(); if that fails, avoid setting DataFrame.columns to prevent pandas length-mismatch exceptions.
- Hardcoded values/constants: no numeric constants need changing. The problematic hardcoded "source of truth" is the use of self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) to derive transformer order; that assumption is what changes. Separator "__" used by _add_prefix_for_feature_names_out is preserved.
- New methods/overrides: none required. Change is limited to three existing internal methods. Remaining gap: we did not run runtime tests; add unit tests reproducing Pipeline.set_output("pandas") + empty-selection transformer to verify fix.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Name/column alignment mismatch in pandas output (root cause)
What: ColumnTransformer._hstack builds transformer_names from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) but reads feature_names_outs from the actual outputs Xs (X.columns). These two sources can be inconsistent, so zip(transformer_names, feature_names_outs) can pair unequal-length lists and then output.columns = names_out raises pandas ValueError "Length mismatch".  
Where: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py : ColumnTransformer._hstack. (Checkpoint 1)

### Finding 2: The same wrong assumption used elsewhere (affects index/slicing)
What: _validate_output and _record_output_indices also assume the sequence from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) lines up with the Xs produced by transformation; they can therefore compute wrong validation or wrong output_indices_ slices when the assumption fails.  
Where: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py : ColumnTransformer._validate_output, ColumnTransformer._record_output_indices. (Checkpoint 4)

### Finding 3: Practical, minimal fix — use transformers_ as authority and fallbacks
What: Use self.transformers_ (the fitted transformer list set by _update_fitted_transformers) filtered by not (trans == "drop" or _is_empty_column_selection(columns)) to produce the transformer name sequence that aligns with Xs. If lengths still mismatch, try self.get_feature_names_out(); if that fails, do not set output.columns (avoid raising pandas length-mismatch). This change must be applied to _hstack, _validate_output, and _record_output_indices to be complete.  
Where: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py — apply in _hstack, _validate_output, _record_output_indices. (Checkpoint 4)

### Finding 4: Test and interaction gaps to validate
What: The investigation did not run the code or unit tests. We must add a unit test that reproduces: Pipeline([... ColumnTransformer with an empty-selection step ...]).set_output(transform="pandas") and then fit(X, y) — assert no pandas Length mismatch and correct columns. Also add tests for transformers that return DataFrame but do NOT implement get_feature_names_out (existing tests cover parts).  
Where: tests to add: sklearn/compose/tests/test_column_transformer.py (new cases). (Checkpoint 5)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py:ColumnTransformer._hstack
- **Wrong assumption**: It assumes transformer_names = [t[0] for t in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)] lines up 1:1 with feature_names_outs = [X.columns for X in Xs].  
- **Evidence**: Stack trace in bug report ends with output.columns = names_out raising pandas ValueError "Length mismatch: Expected axis has 3 elements, new values have 0 elements". The code at _hstack (observed in file) does:
  - output = pd.concat(Xs, axis=1)
  - transformer_names = [t[0] for t in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)]
  - feature_names_outs = [X.columns for X in Xs]
  - names_out = self._add_prefix_for_feature_names_out(list(zip(transformer_names, feature_names_outs)))
  - output.columns = names_out
  This explicitly zips two sequences that can be unequal and then assigns columns. (Checkpoint 1)
- **Related locations**: ColumnTransformer._validate_output, ColumnTransformer._record_output_indices depend on the same alignment assumption.

### Location: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py:ColumnTransformer._validate_output
- **Wrong assumption**: It computes names via self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) and then does for Xs, name in zip(result, names): ... expecting the name sequence to align with result.  
- **Evidence**: Code block uses names = [ name for name, _, _, _ in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) ] then zips with result; if names shorter than result the last outputs are not validated and alignment is wrong; if names longer, zip truncates and some names are ignored. This is a direct cause of inconsistent validation and later index mapping. (Checkpoint 4)
- **Related locations**: _record_output_indices and _hstack rely on the same sequence.

### Location: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py:ColumnTransformer._record_output_indices
- **Wrong assumption**: It iterates enumerate(self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)) and uses transformer_idx to index Xs (i.e., assumes ordering and count match).  
- **Evidence**: Code uses for transformer_idx, (name, _, _, _) in enumerate(self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)): n_columns = Xs[transformer_idx].shape[1]; self.output_indices_[name] = slice(idx, idx + n_columns). If the iterator omits an entry (empty selection) or insert replacements in a different order than Xs, indexing will be wrong or raise. The bug trace shows the assignment of DataFrame.columns failing because earlier indices/name mapping was inconsistent. (Checkpoint 4)
- **Related locations**: _hstack (where columns are set) and get_feature_names_out (which uses _iter but is a separate path — keep as-is but ensure consistency).

### Location: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py:ColumnTransformer._update_fitted_transformers (evidence location)
- **Wrong assumption (exposed)**: This method stores fitted transformers in self.transformers_ but leaves entries for empty selections (it assigns trans = old for empty selections). That is fine, but it means two different iteration paths (self._iter(fitted=True) vs filtering self.transformers_) yield different views; _hstack used the first one and Xs were produced using the second view.  
- **Evidence**: _update_fitted_transformers implementation uses:
    for name, old, column in self.transformers:
        if _is_empty_column_selection(column):
            trans = old
        else:
            trans = next(fitted_transformers)
        transformers_.append((name, trans, column))
  This preserves empty-selection entries in transformers_. Hence deriving transformer order from transformers_ (filtering empty selections) matches Xs. (Checkpoint 3)
- **Related locations**: _hstack, _validate_output, _record_output_indices must use the same canonical ordering approach.

--- End of evidence locker.

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify each claim in the draft by inspecting the ColumnTransformer source. I fetched sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py and examined the implementations of _hstack, _validate_output, _record_output_indices, _update_fitted_transformers, and _iter to confirm the reported behavior. Below I document what I checked, what I found, and any corrections to the draft that are required.

1) Why I read this file and what I expected to learn
- I needed to confirm whether the draft's diagnosis (mismatch between transformer name sequence and produced outputs) is supported by the code and whether the code paths named in the draft actually use self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) and/or self.transformers_/self.transformers_. I expected to learn:
  - How transformer name lists are built when producing pandas outputs.
  - Whether _iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) skips empty selections.
  - Whether Xs (the list of outputs) can include entries corresponding to empty selections.
  - How transformers_ is populated in _update_fitted_transformers and whether that preserves placeholders for empty selections.

I then inspected the returned file and verified relevant lines; the findings below cite exact line ranges.

2) Verification of each method mentioned in the draft

- ColumnTransformer._hstack
  - Where in file: lines ~824-876; relevant lines:
    - The pandas branch starts at 853: config = _get_output_config("transform", self)
    - If config dense == "pandas": output = pd.concat(Xs, axis=1) (line 855)
    - If verbose_feature_names_out True (line 862), it builds:
      - transformer_names = [t[0] for t in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)] (lines 865-867)
      - feature_names_outs = [X.columns for X in Xs] (line 868)
      - names_out = self._add_prefix_for_feature_names_out(list(zip(transformer_names, feature_names_outs))) (lines 869-871)
      - output.columns = names_out (line 872)
  - Verification: The code indeed constructs transformer_names from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) and feature_names_outs by reading X.columns for each element of Xs, then zips them and assigns as DataFrame columns. This matches the draft's description of the failing assignment point. The specific code lines show the exact sequence the draft flagged.

- ColumnTransformer._validate_output
  - Where in file: lines 609-623:
    - names = [ name for name, _, _, _ in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) ] (line 614-616)
    - for Xs, name in zip(result, names): ... check Xs.ndim == 2 (lines 617-622)
  - Verification: The code builds a names list from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) and zips it with result (the list of transformer outputs). This assumes the two sequences align in length and ordering; the code shows that assumption explicitly.

- ColumnTransformer._record_output_indices
  - Where in file: lines 624-646:
    - for transformer_idx, (name, _, _, _) in enumerate(self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)): (lines 631-633)
    - n_columns = Xs[transformer_idx].shape[1] (line 634)
    - self.output_indices_[name] = slice(idx, idx + n_columns) (line 635)
    - Then a final loop sets empty slices for names not present (lines 641-644)
  - Verification: The method indexes into Xs by transformer_idx, relying on a positional 1:1 match between the results Xs and the iteration order yielded by self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True). The code lines confirm this assumption.

- ColumnTransformer._update_fitted_transformers
  - Where in file: lines 581-608:
    - It iterates for name, old, column, _ in self._iter(): (line 587)
    - If old == "passthrough": it consumes a fitted transformer from the fitted_transformers iterator but sets trans = "passthrough" in the stored tuple and records mapping in self._name_to_fitted_passthrough (lines 590-599)
    - elif _is_empty_column_selection(column): trans = old (line 599-601)
    - At the end it sets self.transformers_ = transformers_ (line 607-608)
  - Verification: _update_fitted_transformers intentionally preserves original 'old' values for empty column selections (it sets trans = old) rather than replacing them with a fitted object. This creates transformer entries in self.transformers_ for empty-selection transformers (they remain as the original specification), as the draft said. The exact lines show this preservation.

- ColumnTransformer._iter behavior (important cross-check)
  - Where in file: lines 347-409:
    - If fitted: if replace_strings True, a branch replaces 'passthrough' with a FunctionTransformer and then:
      - if trans == "drop": continue (line 392-393)
      - elif _is_empty_column_selection(columns): continue (line 394-395)
    - Thus _iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) explicitly skips empty selections and 'drop'.
  - Verification: This is the key divergence: self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) will skip entries whose column selection is empty, while (as shown above) Xs (the outputs produced earlier in fit_transform/transform) can include outputs corresponding to the original transformers including empty selections. The code shows both behaviors clearly.

3) Verify the RootCause claims against code evidence
- The draft's central claim is that ColumnTransformer constructs a transformer-name list from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) and separately constructs feature_names_outs from Xs, and these two lists can differ in length/order, causing assignment of DataFrame.columns to raise a pandas ValueError. This is supported by the code:
  - _hstack uses transformer_names = [t[0] for t in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)] (line 865-867) and feature_names_outs = [X.columns for X in Xs] (line 868), and then zips and assigns output.columns = names_out (lines 869-872).
  - _iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) explicitly skips empty selections (lines 393-395) while Xs come from the earlier _fit_transform run that iterated using self._iter(fitted=False, ...) and did not skip empty selections (see how _fit_transform builds its own transformers list on line 659). The code paths confirm the two sources differ.
- The draft also lists that the same wrong assumption appears in _validate_output and _record_output_indices. The code confirms both methods use self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) to create a names sequence or to enumerate, and then zip/index into result/Xs (lines 614-623 and 631-636 respectively), so the diagnosis is correct.

4) Claims that are not (entirely) supported or need correction
- The draft states: "Therefore self.transformers_ filtered to exclude 'drop' and empty selections is a more accurate authoritative sequence for the actual produced Xs than self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)." This sentence is problematic:
  - It prescribes an authoritative source and a specific filtering step, which is a fix prescription rather than a pure diagnostic statement. Per the instructions, we must not include fixes. Also, it is not strictly accurate as stated: self.transformers_ (as populated by _update_fitted_transformers) preserves placeholders for empty selections (see lines 599-603) and so filtering out empty selections from self.transformers_ would produce a sequence that excludes entries for empty selections — but Xs (the outputs) may include empty outputs corresponding to those original transformers (the list Xs comes from the earlier _fit_transform call that iterated the original transformers, not from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)). In short, the assertion that filtering self.transformers_ yields the authoritative sequence needs careful wording and should not be presented as a fix.
  - I therefore mark that sentence as a prescriptive/inaccurate claim and remove or reword it in the revised RootCause to a factual diagnostic: the code produces different views (one iteration that skips empty selections and one that includes them), and that divergence causes the length/order mismatch.

5) Missing or omitted investigation findings that should be included
- The draft already enumerates the three affected methods (_hstack, _validate_output, _record_output_indices) and the underlying divergence in iteration sources (_iter vs transformers_). That covers the important related locations found in the investigation analysis. I verified those locations exist and are relevant.
- The draft included an explicit formula relationship (transformer_names, feature_names_outs, names_out, output.columns) — this is useful diagnostic information and consistent with the code. Keep it, but avoid prescriptive suggestions.
- The investigation suggested fallback strategies and unit test additions; those are fixes or test changes and must not be included in the RootCause. They belong outside the diagnosis; per instructions we will not add any fix guidance inside the report fields.

6) Impact completeness check (are there other code paths affected?)
- get_feature_names_out uses self._iter(fitted=True) (without replace_strings=True) and uses _get_feature_name_out_for_transformer (lines 470-531). That method pays attention to _is_empty_column_selection and returns None for empty selections (line 479-481). The mismatch described affects only the pandas output branch in _hstack (and the validation/index-recording functions). I checked transform() and fit_transform() flows:
  - fit_transform constructs result and Xs (lines 736-744) and then calls self._update_fitted_transformers(transformers), then _validate_output(Xs), _record_output_indices(Xs), and finally _hstack(list(Xs)) (lines 756-760). That ordering shows the Xs list is built before transformers_ is set, and Xs reflects the outputs that correspond to the original iteration used during fitting (which included empty selections). _hstack uses a different iteration path to get names (replace_strings=True) that skips empty selections. This is a consistent code-level explanation of the mismatch.
- All claims about other classes or hardcoded constants are not present — the issue is specifically about iteration/source-of-truth differences in ColumnTransformer. No additional classes were found that change this diagnosis.

7) Field discipline check and edits required
- Description: the draft's Description is acceptable as a concise summary.
- RootCause: requires editing to remove fix proposals (e.g., "The authoritative mapping ... should be derived ..." is a prescription) and to rephrase to strictly state incorrect assumptions and the places they occur. The RootCause must:

  - Not prescribe code changes.
- StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, ObservedBehavior: these are fine; I will keep them mostly unchanged but may tighten wording to remain purely user-visible behaviors.

bug_report.Title: ColumnTransformer pandas output fails when a transformer has zero selected features (Length mismatch)
bug_report.Description: When a ColumnTransformer is used with set_output(transform="pandas") and one of its transformers selects 0 columns (empty selection), fitting a Pipeline that contains this ColumnTransformer can raise a pandas ValueError due to a mismatch when assigning DataFrame.columns. The failure arises while ColumnTransformer assembles a pandas DataFrame from transformer outputs.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) ColumnTransformer._hstack builds a list of transformer names from self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) but builds feature name lists from the actual transformer outputs Xs (X.columns). Specifically, the code does:
   - transformer_names = [t[0] for t in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)]
   - feature_names_outs = [X.columns for X in Xs]
   - names_out = self._add_prefix_for_feature_names_out(list(zip(transformer_names, feature_names_outs)))
   - output.columns = names_out
   This encodes the assumption that transformer_names and feature_names_outs have the same length and ordering. In the code, self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) will skip transformers whose column selection is empty (it contains an explicit continue for _is_empty_column_selection(columns)), while Xs are produced earlier in fit_transform/transform from an iteration that can include outputs for empty selections. When these two sources differ in length/order, assigning output.columns raises a pandas ValueError (length mismatch). (location: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py: ColumnTransformer._hstack, lines ~853-873).

2) ColumnTransformer._validate_output uses the same iterator source and zips it with the produced outputs (result). The code builds names = [name for name, _, _, _ in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)] and then does for Xs, name in zip(result, names): ... which assumes the two sequences align. If they differ due to empty selections being skipped by the iterator, validation is misaligned. (location: sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py: ColumnTransformer._validate_output, lines ~614-623).

"""
Multi-class / multi-label utility function
==========================================

"""
from collections.abc import Sequence
from itertools import chain
import warnings

from scipy.sparse import issparse
from scipy.sparse import dok_matrix
from scipy.sparse import lil_matrix

import numpy as np

from .validation import check_array, _assert_all_finite
from ..utils._array_api import get_namespace


def _unique_multiclass(y):
    xp, is_array_api = get_namespace(y)
    if hasattr(y, "__array__") or is_array_api:
        return xp.unique_values(xp.asarray(y))
    else:
        return set(y)


def _unique_indicator(y):
    return np.arange(
        check_array(y, input_name="y", accept_sparse=["csr", "csc", "coo"]).shape[1]
    )


_FN_UNIQUE_LABELS = {
    "binary": _unique_multiclass,
    "multiclass": _unique_multiclass,
    "multilabel-indicator": _unique_indicator,
}


def unique_labels(*ys):
    """Extract an ordered array of unique labels.

    We don't allow:
        - mix of multilabel and multiclass (single label) targets
        - mix of label indicator matrix and anything else,
          because there are no explicit labels)
        - mix of label indicator matrices of different sizes
        - mix of string and integer labels

    At the moment, we also don't allow "multiclass-multioutput" input type.

    Parameters
    ----------
    *ys : array-likes
        Label values.

    Returns
    -------
    out : ndarray of shape (n_unique_labels,)
        An ordered array of unique labels.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from sklearn.utils.multiclass import unique_labels
    >>> unique_labels([3, 5, 5, 5, 7, 7])
    array([3, 5, 7])
    >>> unique_labels([1, 2, 3, 4], [2, 2, 3, 4])
    array([1, 2, 3, 4])
    >>> unique_labels([1, 2, 10], [5, 11])
    array([ 1,  2,  5, 10, 11])
    """
    xp, is_array_api = get_namespace(*ys)
    if not ys:
        raise ValueError("No argument has been passed.")
    # Check that we don't mix label format

    ys_types = set(type_of_target(x) for x in ys)
    if ys_types == {"binary", "multiclass"}:
        ys_types = {"multiclass"}

    if len(ys_types) > 1:
        raise ValueError("Mix type of y not allowed, got types %s" % ys_types)

    label_type = ys_types.pop()

    # Check consistency for the indicator format
    if (
        label_type == "multilabel-indicator"
        and len(
            set(
                check_array(y, accept_sparse=["csr", "csc", "coo"]).shape[1] for y in ys
            )
        )
        > 1
    ):
        raise ValueError(
            "Multi-label binary indicator input with different numbers of labels"
        )

    # Get the unique set of labels
    _unique_labels = _FN_UNIQUE_LABELS.get(label_type, None)
    if not _unique_labels:
        raise ValueError("Unknown label type: %s" % repr(ys))

    if is_array_api:
        # array_api does not allow for mixed dtypes
        unique_ys = xp.concat([_unique_labels(y) for y in ys])
        return xp.unique_values(unique_ys)

    ys_labels = set(chain.from_iterable((i for i in _unique_labels(y)) for y in ys))
    # Check that we don't mix string type with number type
    if len(set(isinstance(label, str) for label in ys_labels)) > 1:
        raise ValueError("Mix of label input types (string and number)")

    return xp.asarray(sorted(ys_labels))


def _is_integral_float(y):
    return y.dtype.kind == "f" and np.all(y.astype(int) == y)


def is_multilabel(y):
    """Check if ``y`` is in a multilabel format.

    Parameters
    ----------
    y : ndarray of shape (n_samples,)
        Target values.

    Returns
    -------
    out : bool
        Return ``True``, if ``y`` is in a multilabel format, else ```False``.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> from sklearn.utils.multiclass import is_multilabel
    >>> is_multilabel([0, 1, 0, 1])
    False
    >>> is_multilabel([[1], [0, 2], []])
    False
    >>> is_multilabel(np.array([[1, 0], [0, 0]]))
    True
    >>> is_multilabel(np.array([[1], [0], [0]]))
    False
    >>> is_multilabel(np.array([[1, 0, 0]]))
    True
    """
    xp, is_array_api = get_namespace(y)
    if hasattr(y, "__array__") or isinstance(y, Sequence) or is_array_api:
        # DeprecationWarning will be replaced by ValueError, see NEP 34
        # https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0034-infer-dtype-is-object.html
        with warnings.catch_warnings():
            warnings.simplefilter("error", np.VisibleDeprecationWarning)
            try:
                y = xp.asarray(y)
            except (np.VisibleDeprecationWarning, ValueError):
                # dtype=object should be provided explicitly for ragged arrays,
                # see NEP 34
                y = xp.asarray(y, dtype=object)

    if not (hasattr(y, "shape") and y.ndim == 2 and y.shape[1] > 1):
        return False

    if issparse(y):
        if isinstance(y, (dok_matrix, lil_matrix)):
            y = y.tocsr()
        labels = xp.unique_values(y.data)
        return (
            len(y.data) == 0
            or (labels.size == 1 or (labels.size == 2) and (0 in labels))
            and (y.dtype.kind in "biu" or _is_integral_float(labels))  # bool, int, uint
        )
    else:
        labels = xp.unique_values(y)

        return len(labels) < 3 and (
            y.dtype.kind in "biu" or _is_integral_float(labels)  # bool, int, uint
        )


def check_classification_targets(y):
    """Ensure that target y is of a non-regression type.

    Only the following target types (as defined in type_of_target) are allowed:
        'binary', 'multiclass', 'multiclass-multioutput',
        'multilabel-indicator', 'multilabel-sequences'

    Parameters
    ----------
    y : array-like
        Target values.
    """
    y_type = type_of_target(y, input_name="y")
    if y_type not in [
        "binary",
        "multiclass",
        "multiclass-multioutput",
        "multilabel-indicator",
        "multilabel-sequences",
    ]:
        raise ValueError("Unknown label type: %r" % y_type)


def type_of_target(y, input_name=""):
    """Determine the type of data indicated by the target.

    Note that this type is the most specific type that can be inferred.
    For example:

        * ``binary`` is more specific but compatible with ``multiclass``.
        * ``multiclass`` of integers is more specific but compatible with
          ``continuous``.
        * ``multilabel-indicator`` is more specific but compatible with
          ``multiclass-multioutput``.

    Parameters
    ----------
    y : {array-like, sparse matrix}
        Target values. If a sparse matrix, `y` is expected to be a
        CSR/CSC matrix.

    input_name : str, default=""
        The data name used to construct the error message.

        .. versionadded:: 1.1.0

    Returns
    -------
    target_type : str
        One of:

        * 'continuous': `y` is an array-like of floats that are not all
          integers, and is 1d or a column vector.
        * 'continuous-multioutput': `y` is a 2d array of floats that are
          not all integers, and both dimensions are of size > 1.
        * 'binary': `y` contains <= 2 discrete values and is 1d or a column
          vector.
        * 'multiclass': `y` contains more than two discrete values, is not a
          sequence of sequences, and is 1d or a column vector.
        * 'multiclass-multioutput': `y` is a 2d array that contains more
          than two discrete values, is not a sequence of sequences, and both
          dimensions are of size > 1.
        * 'multilabel-indicator': `y` is a label indicator matrix, an array
          of two dimensions with at least two columns, and at most 2 unique
          values.
        * 'unknown': `y` is array-like but none of the above, such as a 3d
          array, sequence of sequences, or an array of non-sequence objects.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from sklearn.utils.multiclass import type_of_target
    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> type_of_target([0.1, 0.6])
    'continuous'
    >>> type_of_target([1, -1, -1, 1])
    'binary'
    >>> type_of_target(['a', 'b', 'a'])
    'binary'
    >>> type_of_target([1.0, 2.0])
    'binary'
    >>> type_of_target([1, 0, 2])
    'multiclass'
    >>> type_of_target([1.0, 0.0, 3.0])
    'multiclass'
    >>> type_of_target(['a', 'b', 'c'])
    'multiclass'
    >>> type_of_target(np.array([[1, 2], [3, 1]]))
    'multiclass-multioutput'
    >>> type_of_target([[1, 2]])
    'multilabel-indicator'
    >>> type_of_target(np.array([[1.5, 2.0], [3.0, 1.6]]))
    'continuous-multioutput'
    >>> type_of_target(np.array([[0, 1], [1, 1]]))
    'multilabel-indicator'
    """
    xp, is_array_api = get_namespace(y)
    valid = (
        (isinstance(y, Sequence) or issparse(y) or hasattr(y, "__array__"))
        and not isinstance(y, str)
        or is_array_api
    )

    if not valid:
        raise ValueError(
            "Expected array-like (array or non-string sequence), got %r" % y

    sparse_pandas = y.__class__.__name__ in ["SparseSeries", "SparseArray"]
    if sparse_pandas:

    if is_multilabel(y):

    # DeprecationWarning will be replaced by ValueError, see NEP 34
    # https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0034-infer-dtype-is-object.html
    # We therefore catch both deprecation (NumPy < 1.24) warning and
    # value error (NumPy >= 1.24).
    with warnings.catch_warnings():

        if not issparse(y):
            try:
                y = xp.asarray(y)
            except (np.VisibleDeprecationWarning, ValueError):

                # see NEP 34

    try:

            and not isinstance(y[0], str)
        ):

                "You appear to be using a legacy multi-label data"
                " representation. Sequence of sequences are no"
                " longer supported; use a binary array or sparse"
                " matrix instead - the MultiLabelBinarizer"

from contextlib import suppress
from collections import Counter
from typing import NamedTuple

import numpy as np
from . import is_scalar_nan


def _unique(values, *, return_inverse=False, return_counts=False):
    """Helper function to find unique values with support for python objects.

    Uses pure python method for object dtype, and numpy method for
    all other dtypes.

    Parameters
    ----------
    values : ndarray
        Values to check for unknowns.

    return_inverse : bool, default=False
        If True, also return the indices of the unique values.

    return_counts : bool, default=False
        If True, also return the number of times each unique item appears in
        values.

    Returns
    -------
    unique : ndarray
        The sorted unique values.

    unique_inverse : ndarray
        The indices to reconstruct the original array from the unique array.
        Only provided if `return_inverse` is True.

    unique_counts : ndarray
        The number of times each of the unique values comes up in the original
        array. Only provided if `return_counts` is True.
    """
    if values.dtype == object:
        return _unique_python(
            values, return_inverse=return_inverse, return_counts=return_counts
        )
    # numerical
    return _unique_np(
        values, return_inverse=return_inverse, return_counts=return_counts
    )


def _unique_np(values, return_inverse=False, return_counts=False):
    """Helper function to find unique values for numpy arrays that correctly
    accounts for nans. See `_unique` documentation for details."""
    uniques = np.unique(
        values, return_inverse=return_inverse, return_counts=return_counts
    )

    inverse, counts = None, None

    if return_counts:
        *uniques, counts = uniques

    if return_inverse:
        *uniques, inverse = uniques

    if return_counts or return_inverse:
        uniques = uniques[0]

    # np.unique will have duplicate missing values at the end of `uniques`
    # here we clip the nans and remove it from uniques
    if uniques.size and is_scalar_nan(uniques[-1]):
        nan_idx = np.searchsorted(uniques, np.nan)
        uniques = uniques[: nan_idx + 1]
        if return_inverse:
            inverse[inverse > nan_idx] = nan_idx

        if return_counts:
            counts[nan_idx] = np.sum(counts[nan_idx:])
            counts = counts[: nan_idx + 1]

    ret = (uniques,)

    if return_inverse:
        ret += (inverse,)

    if return_counts:
        ret += (counts,)

    return ret[0] if len(ret) == 1 else ret


class MissingValues(NamedTuple):
    """Data class for missing data information"""

    nan: bool
    none: bool

    def to_list(self):
        """Convert tuple to a list where None is always first."""
        output = []
        if self.none:
            output.append(None)
        if self.nan:
            output.append(np.nan)
        return output


def _extract_missing(values):
    """Extract missing values from `values`.

    Parameters
    ----------
    values: set
        Set of values to extract missing from.

    Returns
    -------
    output: set
        Set with missing values extracted.

    missing_values: MissingValues
        Object with missing value information.
    """
    missing_values_set = {
        value for value in values if value is None or is_scalar_nan(value)
    }

    if not missing_values_set:
        return values, MissingValues(nan=False, none=False)

    if None in missing_values_set:
        if len(missing_values_set) == 1:
            output_missing_values = MissingValues(nan=False, none=True)
        else:
            # If there is more than one missing value, then it has to be
            # float('nan') or np.nan
            output_missing_values = MissingValues(nan=True, none=True)
    else:
        output_missing_values = MissingValues(nan=True, none=False)

    # create set without the missing values
    output = values - missing_values_set
    return output, output_missing_values


class _nandict(dict):
    """Dictionary with support for nans."""

    def __init__(self, mapping):
        super().__init__(mapping)
        for key, value in mapping.items():
            if is_scalar_nan(key):
                self.nan_value = value
                break

    def __missing__(self, key):
        if hasattr(self, "nan_value") and is_scalar_nan(key):
            return self.nan_value
        raise KeyError(key)

    table = _nandict({val: i for i, val in enumerate(uniques)})
    return np.array([table[v] for v in values])

```python
    import pandas as pd

        y_predicted = pd.Series([0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1], dtype="int64")

        unique_labels(y_true, y_predicted)
```

Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

Our current workaround is to convert the data to numpy arrays with the corresponding dtype that works prior to passing it into `unique_labels`.

Summary of findings up front
- Root cause: type_of_target converts pandas nullable-extension dtype Series (e.g., dtype "Int64", "Float64", "boolean") to a numpy object ndarray via xp.asarray(y). The code later treats object dtype arrays whose first element is not a str as "unknown" (at line 343), causing mix-type detection in unique_labels to fail and raising "Mix type of y not allowed, got types {..., 'unknown'}".
- Primary function responsible: type_of_target (sklearn/utils/multiclass.py).
- Fix approach (high level): detect pandas nullable extension dtypes early in type_of_target (without importing pandas at module import time), and convert them to an appropriate NumPy dtype array before the dtype-based checks. The conversion should be careful about missing values (pd.NA) and preserve expected behavior: when there are no missing values convert to integer/float/bool numpy dtypes, otherwise convert to float (for integers with missing) or float with np.nan (for floats) or object (for booleans with pd.NA) as appropriate — and ensure symmetry with any complementary methods.

If xp.asarray yields an object dtype ndarray whose first element is not str, the function returns "unknown" (line 343).

Proposed code change (high-level)
- Modify type_of_target to detect pandas nullable extension dtypes early and coerce them to an appropriate NumPy ndarray with a numeric dtype where possible before the xp.asarray(...) check that later leads to object dtype handling.
- The detection should be dynamic and not import pandas at module import time. It should try to import pandas lazily and only when needed.
- Conversion rules (with explanation and defensive behavior):
  1. If the input y has an attribute dtype and that dtype has a name in {"Int64", "Float64", "boolean"} (the pandas nullable dtypes reported), then:
     - For "Int64":
       - If y has no missing values (y.isna().any() is False), convert to numpy int64 via y.to_numpy(dtype="int64") or y.astype("int64").to_numpy() to get np.int64 dtype.
       - If y has missing values, convert to numpy float64 via y.to_numpy(dtype="float64") so pd.NA becomes np.nan (this preserves ability for continuous checks; integers with NA become float with NaN).
     - For "Float64":
       - Convert to numpy float64 via y.to_numpy(dtype="float64") — pd.NA becomes np.nan.
     - For "boolean":
       - If y has no missing values, convert to numpy bool via y.to_numpy(dtype="bool").
       - If missing values present, convert to object or float? I propose converting to numpy object (y.to_numpy(dtype=object)) to preserve pd.NA or convert to float? Here, boolean with missing is tricky because there is no common numpy typed dtype with missing bool. Several options:
         - convert to object and let later code handle it (but object will trigger unknown if first element is not str).
         - convert to numpy float where True->1.0, False->0.0, pd.NA->np.nan. That may be preferable because many sklearn functions expect numeric types and treat floats with NaN as "continuous"; but for classification that might be unexpected. However, the bug report focused on boolean Series without missing values, so simplest safe approach is:
           - If boolean has no missing values: convert to numpy bool.
           - If boolean has missing values: convert to numpy object but ensure we don't return "unknown" because first element might be True/False or pd.NA. We can avoid the "unknown" branch by converting pd.NA to np.nan and then mapping to float? For minimal change, converting to object and then catching object with non-str first element is not good (would still lead to unknown). Better convert boolean-with-missing to numpy float64 (True->1.0, False->0.0, NA->np.nan). This makes the dtype.kind == 'f' and then continuous/multiclass checks proceed (it will be considered continuous if non-integer float values; but since True/False->1/0 integer floats, code path for integer float check will detect integral floats if all floats are integers — then behavior will be consistent with integer).
- Where to place the detection: in type_of_target before is_multilabel? Or right after obtaining xp, is_array_api and before is_multilabel conversion that also calls xp.asarray? We need to ensure is_multilabel properly treats these inputs; since is_multilabel also calls xp.asarray and later checks y.dtype.kind, the safe place is at the very start of type_of_target and also mirrored inside is_multilabel (or else make a single helper that both functions can use). To keep change minimal and correct, I will:
  - Insert a small helper conversion at the top of type_of_target immediately after the get_namespace(...) and valid checks (before calling is_multilabel), converting pandas nullable dtypes to numpy arrays as described.
  - Also insert the same helper at the top of is_multilabel (because is_multilabel is used independently by callers, e.g., other code might call is_multilabel directly). However, note that in type_of_target we call is_multilabel(y) before the xp.asarray conversion block (see lines ~298). If we convert y to an ndarray before calling is_multilabel, then is_multilabel will get a numpy array and behave properly; but other call sites may call is_multilabel with a pandas Series directly. So we must also update is_multilabel to handle pandas nullable dtypes. Safer to add the conversion logic in both is_multilabel and type_of_target (avoid changing every caller).
- Avoid heavy imports and keep code robust: try/except around import pandas; if pandas isn't installed, do nothing. Use attribute checks rather than type checks to avoid hard dependency.

Concrete code patch (pseudocode to be applied in sklearn/utils/multiclass.py)
Insert a small helper function inside the module (private) or inline code in both functions:

def _coerce_pandas_nullable(y):
    """If y is a pandas Series or Index with pandas nullable dtypes,
    coerce it to an appropriate numpy ndarray to allow dtype.kind checks.

    Returns the possibly converted y (if conversion performed), otherwise original y.
    """
    try:
        import pandas as pd
    except Exception:
        return y

    # Only proceed for pandas Series, Index, or ExtensionArray-like objects
    dtype = getattr(y, "dtype", None)
    if dtype is None:
        return y

    name = getattr(dtype, "name", None)
    if name not in {"Int64", "Float64", "boolean"}:
        return y

    # For Series/Index: we can inspect missing values via isna()
    has_isna = hasattr(y, "isna")
    has_to_numpy = hasattr(y, "to_numpy")

    try:
        if name == "Int64":
            if has_isna and not y.isna().any():
                return y.to_numpy(dtype="int64")
            else:
                # convert to float for NA->np.nan
                return y.to_numpy(dtype="float64")
        elif name == "Float64":
            return y.to_numpy(dtype="float64")
        elif name == "boolean":
            if has_isna and not y.isna().any():
                return y.to_numpy(dtype="bool")
            else:
                # Map to float: True->1.0, False->0.0, NA->np.nan
                # Use to_numpy dtype float to get np.nan for pd.NA
                # (pandas will convert booleans to float with NaN)
                return y.to_numpy(dtype="float64")
    except Exception:
        # Be conservative: if conversion fails, keep y unchanged
        return y

Then in type_of_target, near line ~282 after get_namespace and valid checks, before calling is_multilabel(y), add:

# Coerce pandas nullable dtypes to NumPy arrays to preserve dtype.kind handling
y = _coerce_pandas_nullable(y)

Also add the same call at the beginning of is_multilabel (immediately after xp, is_array_api = get_namespace(y)):

y = _coerce_pandas_nullable(y)

This ensures both functions see numeric numpy arrays for pandas nullable types and avoids the object dtype branch that returns "unknown".

Now the checklist to challenge this fix:

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Current code assumes that xp.asarray(y) will yield a numpy dtype that reflects the true underlying data kind for dtype-kind checks (i.e., that the value of y.dtype.kind will be meaningful). The code also implicitly assumes inputs are either numpy arrays with numeric dtypes or sequences that convert cleanly. The fix changes the assumption by explicitly handling pandas nullable extension dtypes and coercing them to numeric numpy arrays (or float arrays when missing values exist) so that dtype.kind checks work as intended.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The critical hardcoded values / checks in the module that rely on dtype.kind:
  - y.dtype == object and not isinstance(y.flat[0], str) (line 343) — encodes that object dtype indicates weird object elements (since for pandas extension arrays xp.asarray yields object). This check is what makes pandas nullable dtypes return "unknown".
    - Under the new behavior, if we coerce nullable dtypes to numeric numpy arrays, this check will not hit (y.dtype != object).
  - dtype.kind checks in is_multilabel and type_of_target:
    - y.dtype.kind == "f" (line 354) — floats.
    - y.dtype.kind in "biu" (line 177 and 183) — bool, int, uint.
  - The explicit set of extension dtype names {"Int64", "Float64", "boolean"} — these are the values we check for; we keep them exact because the bug report specifically mentions these. If the assumption that these are the only extension dtypes of interest changes (e.g., more extension dtypes are added), we would need to extend the set. Also, conversion rules above were chosen because there is no direct numpy dtype mapping for pandas' extension type with missing values.
- Are these values still correct if assumption changed?:
  - If pandas adds more nullable types or variant names, our explicit set would need extending.
  - Converting Int64 with missing to float64 encodes the assumption that missing integers are representable as float with np.nan. This is a common pattern (pandas often uses float as a fallback), but we must acknowledge this changes the dtype for inputs with missing values. However, this is safer than leaving as object and returning "unknown". The change is acceptable because sklearn already often treats NaNs (float NaNs) specially and functions using type_of_target are only to classify the kind of target — missing values typically cause other validation checks downstream.
- Conclusion: These hardcoded strings and the y.dtype checks must be left as they are except we add the conversion to avoid y.dtype==object. The set of nullable dtype names must be maintained if more are desired in the future.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- If any code writes or serializes labels based on dtype assumptions, we must ensure symmetry. The main "reverse" operations to consider are:
  - Any code that consumes y after type_of_target uses type information to drive behavior (e.g., unique_labels uses label_type to choose unique extraction). Our change doesn't alter the semantics for non-missing data: Int64/Float64/boolean with no missing values will be converted to numpy int64/float64/bool so unique_labels will behave identically to native numpy dtypes.

In type_of_target the function may then classify y as 'continuous' if it sees floats with non-integer parts (not the case here) or as 'binary'/'multiclass' otherwise. For integer columns with NA -> float with NaN, if rest of values are integral floats, the code later checks xp.unique_values(y) and dtype.kind == 'f' and uses _is_integral_float(labels) to detect integral floats (line 353 onwards). The function uses that to consider such arrays as discrete numeric classes as well.
  - We should re-check the parent's complementary methods (if any) — but here type_of_target is a leaf-level classifier. Unique_labels and other callers that rely on type_of_target will still work because they use the returned label_type. The conversions we've proposed don't change the label_type for the typical cases without NA. For NA-cases, behavior will be consistent with existing sklearn handling of np.nan.
- Concrete tracing (per instructions) for parent's methods that perform complementary operations:
  - unique_labels: It calls type_of_target(y) to decide label_type, then calls _FN_UNIQUE_LABELS[label_type](y) to obtain unique labels. We must ensure that after converting to numpy arrays we still pass the right object to _unique_multiclass or _unique_indicator. Since _unique_multiclass uses xp.unique_values(xp.asarray(y)), if we pass in a numpy array already, xp.asarray will not change dtype and xp.unique_values will behave as intended.
  - So the reverse (writing) path is safe.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Check each method in the module that we read:
  - unique_labels: will work — type_of_target now recognizes pandas nullable dtypes as numeric (except when missing values cause fallback to float), avoiding "unknown", so unique_labels will not raise the mixed-type ValueError in the reported scenario.
  - is_multilabel: we must also add the same coercion (or ensure type_of_target coerces before calling is_multilabel). But is_multilabel can be called independently elsewhere; therefore we must add the same logic at the top of is_multilabel as well. After adding conversion in both functions, is_multilabel's dtype.kind checks operate on numpy arrays and will work.
  - _unique_multiclass and _unique_indicator: unaffected (they rely on xp.unique_values and check_array).
  - _is_integral_float and other helpers: unaffected, but they will receive numeric arrays when appropriate.
- If we only changed type_of_target and not is_multilabel, then is_multilabel when called directly (outside of type_of_target) would still see pandas nullable dtypes and could behave incorrectly. Therefore the fix must include updating is_multilabel as well. This addresses the "Does every existing method still work?" question.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- I proposed adding a small private helper function _coerce_pandas_nullable(y) to centralize the conversion logic. This is a new method (module-private).

If you’d like, I can now:
